al.idx-hero-title:
Aldric von Reichel
for Cambria
al.idx-hero-sub: A campaign for the working masses. I am fighting for a Cambria where every small business can thrive and every rural town is connected - built on the unbreakable foundations of our republic.
al.idx-vision-desc: Our campaign is built on tangible commitments to the people of Cambria. From defending local businesses against monopolies and securing fair contracts, to investing in critical rail infrastructure and agricultural research, we are fighting for an economy that empowers everyone.
al.idx-b1-d: Mandatory reviews for mergers to protect local businesses.
al.idx-b2-d: Sector minimums to prevent predatory agreements.
al.idx-b3-d: Dedicated connectivity built with Cambria's zoning authority.
al.idx-b4-d: Regional research prioritizing local soil, crops, and talent.
al.idx-b5-d: Timed to local harvest and production cycles.
al.idx-b6-d: Legal safeguards protecting the right to organize.
al.plat-desc: We are building an economy that rewards those who build it, by hand and by brain, ending the exploitation by monopolies.
al.plat-b1-d: Automatic review triggers for mergers to protect local suppliers.
al.plat-b2-d: Minimum terms by sector preventing predatory agreements.
al.plat-b3-d: Dedicated connection retaining local zoning authority.
al.plat-b4-d: Regional center prioritizing local soil, crops, and talent.
al.plat-b5-d: Timed to local harvest and production cycles.
al.plat-b6-d: Real enforcement behind the right to organize and strike.
al.plat-b1-md: "People who'd actually notice three delivery firms becoming one, before the paperwork was finalized instead of after, when it is late to fix it... This law would establish the door into monopolies slower. And a slower door is time you don't currently have."

A buyer can still choose not to work with you. They cannot use the absence of alternatives as leverage to set terms below that floor."

Funded positions here, reserved for Cambrian applicants first, with the university stipends to make it possible for someone from this town to train and come home to something worth coming home to."

A stop that exists because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, or so I believe your cargo is worth stopping for."

al.ev-desc: Follow Aldric von Reichel on the campaign trail as we bring the platform to every corner of Cambria.
al.ev-card1-d: Aldric von Reichel meets with Governess Wren and local shop owners at the guildhall. He outlines his platform for anti-monopoly enforcement, regional contract protections, and bringing a dedicated R&D center directly to Cambria.
al.ev-card2-d: Speaking with workers in the field and locals at the town well, Aldric details the High-Speed Rail node plan. He commits to ensuring the node connects Cambria without surrendering local zoning authority to larger districts.
al.gh-sub: Aldric von Reichel visits the guildhall of Cambria.
al.gh-p1: The guildhall smelled of old paper, the kind of smell that came from a building that has been actively used by the local community. Aldric von Reichel had walked the market street before coming inside, four shopfronts without any cameras trailing him. A shoemaker had shown him a boot he was proud of. A grocer had complained about delivery contracts that always seemed to favor whoever already had the most trucks. By the time he reached the guildhall, he had heard the shape of the argument he was about to have before anyone in the room had said a word.
al.gh-p2: The local council of shop owners sat around a long table that had clearly hosted disputes longer and pettier than this one. Twelve chairs, most of them full. At the head sat Governess Wren, a woman who ran the town's only hardware supplier and, by unspoken consensus, ran most other things too.
al.gh-p3: "Mr. von Reichel," she said, not rising. "You understand we've heard the word 'union' spoken with great affection by your party before. Some of us in this room employ three people. Some of us employ one, ourselves. We are not sure your affection extends to us."
al.gh-p4: A murmur of agreement went around the table. Aldric remained standing, hands loose at his sides, no notes.
al.gh-p5: "It doesn't," he said. "Not in the way you mean it. I am not here to organize your one employee against you, Governess. I am here because I think the thing crushing your margins and the thing crushing a factory floor worker's wages in Cambria is, more often than not, the same thing wearing two different coats."
al.gh-p6: A man near the far end, younger with a hardware apron still on, came forward.
al.gh-p7: "Go on, then," he said. "Name the reason."
al.gh-p8: "Consolidation," Aldric said. "Three delivery firms in this region ten years ago. One now. You all know why your contracts stopped being negotiations and turned into their own terms handed down from a corporation building in Montiablo you've never seen. That is monopoly, not to be confused with a fair free market, and monopoly does not care whether the hand it closes belongs to a shop owner or a shift worker. It closes on both."
al.gh-p9: Governess Wren still not fully satisfied proceeded to ask.
al.gh-p10: "So what do you propose," she asked. "Beyond a cause we already knew."
al.gh-p11: "Anti-monopoly enforcement with teeth. Contract protections so a supplier can't be strong-armed by a single buyer with regional leverage. And investment that comes to Cambria instead of past it."
al.gh-p12: An older woman two seats down from Governess Wren, current owner of the town's textile cooperative, set down her pen.
al.gh-p13: "Enforcement with teeth," she said. "We've heard that phrase from three different administrations. What does it actually mean? A commission that meets twice a year and writes a report nobody reads? Or something that would have stopped what happened to the delivery firms before it happened?"
al.gh-p14: Aldric turned to face her directly.
al.gh-p15: "Before," he said. "a merger or buyout above a set threshold in any sector serving a rural region triggers automatic review, which is different from a simple request. Requests can be quietly avoided by filing under a different registry. Additionally, the review board isn't seated entirely in Montiablo. Regional representatives sit on it. People who'd actually notice three delivery firms becoming one, before the paperwork was finalized instead of after, when it is late to fix it."
al.gh-p16: The textile cooperative woman considered this, she was no longer dismissive.
al.gh-p17: "And if the board finds against a merger," she said, "what stops the buyer from simply waiting a season and trying again with better lawyers?"
al.gh-p18: "Nothing, fully," Aldric admitted. "I won't stand here and tell you a law makes a determined monopolist disappear. But every delay is a season your suppliers keep leverage, you keep alternatives, keep the ability to organize a cooperative buying terms of their own instead of being cornered into one buyer's terms. This law would establish the door into monopolies slower. And a slower door is time you don't currently have."
al.gh-p19: A heavyset man who had said nothing so far, seated near Wren's right hand finally spoke.
al.gh-p20: "Contract protections," he said. "Say it plainly. Right now if the one buyer in the region decides my terms next season, I sign or I lose the account entirely, there's no third option. What changes under you?"
al.gh-p21: "A floor," Aldric said. "Minimum contract terms set by sector, renegotiated with regional input rather than dictated by whoever has the largest share of the route in question. A buyer can still choose not to work with you. They cannot use the absence of alternatives as leverage to set terms below that floor. It removes the worst version of the threat, the negotiation held at your throat."
al.gh-p22: The man grunted, expressing neither complete agreement nor total dismissal.
al.gh-p23: "Investment," the man in the apron repeated "Everyone promises investment. Then it's a rail line that runs through us on the way to somewhere else."
al.gh-p25: "That's fair, and it's the right thing to be suspicious of," he said. "So let me be specific instead of comforting. The high-speed line connects Caprica's regions to each other, however Cambria gets its own node, rather than just a platform on someone else's route. Your town stays as the destination."
al.gh-p26: He paused, then added the second half, the part he'd been saving.
al.gh-p27: "And alongside it, a research center. Here. In Cambria, not a simple lecture hall built by Montiablo and staffed by people who know nothing about you and your living. Cambrian scientists, working on what Cambria actually needs, your soil, your supply routes, your workshops, with the freedom to set that agenda themselves. An institution that answers to this town and its people before it answers to anyone else."
al.gh-p28: The cobbler he'd spoken to earlier that morning, invited to sit in, spoke up from a chair near the back.
al.gh-p29: "Staffed by Cambrians," he said. "How? You can't conjure scientists out of a market street. Half the young people here leave for Montiablo the day they're old enough to and don't come back."
al.gh-p30: Aldric thought for a second and responded.
al.gh-p31: "That's exactly why the order matters," he said. "Funded positions here, reserved for Cambrian applicants first, with the university stipends to make it possible for someone from this town to train and come home to something worth coming home to. Right now the incentive runs one direction, leave, and everything worth having is in Montiablo. This reverses that, even slightly. It gives your children a reason to fill out the application from here instead of in a bigger city."
al.gh-p32: He continued, before anyone could ask the obvious follow-up.
al.gh-p33: "And on the rail node, since I promised specifics," he said. "It's not a platform someone builds and forgets. Freight capacity for the feed exchange and the textile cooperative both, timed to your harvest and production cycles rather than a fixed citywide schedule designed around the convenience of others. A stop that exists because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, or so I believe your cargo is worth stopping for."
al.gh-p34: The feed and grain man's eyebrows rose slightly at that.
al.gh-p35: "Timed to harvest," he repeated. "Not the others’ schedule stapled onto us."
al.gh-p36: "Not stapled onto you," Aldric confirmed. "Built around you."
al.gh-p38: "You understand," she said, "that we have been promised things before, and the people who promised them stopped visiting once the ballots were counted."
al.gh-p39: "I know," Aldric said. "I'm not asking you to trust the promise. I'm asking you to hold me to it, in this room, next year, and every year after. That's a better offer than trust. It's accountability. And I promise to visit rural town halls every year, so you may hold me accountable and tell your complaints to my face, instead of sending me mails, which I do not really check."
al.gh-p40: When the meeting broke and the shop owners filed out into the thinning rain, the young man in the apron lingered a moment at the door.
al.gh-p41: "The research center," he said. "The people running it, actually from here?"
al.gh-p43: The man considered this, nodded once.
al.fl-sub: Aldric von Reichel speaks with locals in the fields of Cambria.
al.fl-p1: The field stretched flat and gold and Aldric von Reichel had left the car at the road rather than have it grind down the access track. He walked the last stretch on foot. By the time he reached the workers stringing wire along a fence line, he had already decided not to open with a speech.
al.fl-p2: There was no AVIS or any other news agency crew trailing him or any bodyguards. A rural district didn't want to watch itself being performed to, it wanted to see whether a man would still show up when there was no audience to perform for. The road out here had been unpaved for the last two miles, and the dust on his coat was doing more persuasive work than anything he was about to say.
al.fl-p3: Two men and a woman were working the fence, the kind of task that took a whole morning. The older of the two men, tired, noticed him first and straightened.
al.fl-p4: "You're the one from the papers," he said. "The SDA man. Didn't expect you'd get your boots dirty for it."
al.fl-p5: "Seemed dishonest to talk about Cambria from a stage," Aldric said, "when the actual work is out here."
al.fl-p6: The woman, younger, wiped her hands on her trousers and studied him for a moment.
al.fl-p7: "We heard about the rail line," she said. "Everyone's heard about the rail line. What we haven't heard is whether it stops here or just goes past us on the way to somewhere that matters more."
al.fl-p8: "It stops here," Aldric said. "I can walk you to the exact site, if you've got twenty minutes and don't mind the company."
al.fl-p9: The older man snorted and set down his wire cutters.
al.fl-p10: "Fence isn't going anywhere," he said. "Go on, then. Show us."
al.fl-p11: The proposed node sat a fifteen-minute walk further out, a stretch of open ground marked at its corners with survey stakes and faded orange tape. Aldric stopped at the center of it and turned in a slow half circle.
al.fl-p12: "Here," he said. "Not a platform on the way to one of those big cities. A stop built because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, your grain, your textiles, your goods, and passengers worth carrying both directions instead of just outgoing."
al.fl-p13: "Both directions," the woman repeated. "Meaning what, exactly? A train that stops so someone from the city can look at us on their way through?"
al.fl-p14: "Meaning freight capacity timed to your harvest and production cycles," Aldric said. "And meaning it's cheaper and faster for someone in Cambria to reach opportunity in other places without having to become a resident elsewhere to access it. The connection runs both ways, or it isn't worth building."
al.fl-p15: The older man crouched, picked up a handful of dry soil, and let it run through his fingers, thinking.
al.fl-p16: "And the town itself," he said. "Because a node like this, I've seen what it does to a place. Suddenly everyone wants to build near it, prices climb, and five years later the town isn't the town anymore. It's a suburb with a station."
al.fl-p17: Aldric took a moment before answering, giving it weight.
al.fl-p18: "That's the actual risk," he admitted. "And I won't pretend a rail stop is neutral, it changes a place, always. What I can commit to is that the node connects Cambria to the network without making Cambria's governance answerable to it. No absorption into a larger administrative district built around the station. Zoning and development around the node stay under Cambria's own authority, not handed to a regional planning board seated in the capital. You get the connection. You don't lose the town to it."
al.fl-p19: The woman folded her arms, weighing it.
al.fl-p20: "Promises about zoning are easy to make standing in an empty field," she said, not unkindly. "Harder to keep once the surveyors and the money show up."
al.fl-p21: "Harder," Aldric agreed, "which is exactly why it has to be written into the charter before the first shovel goes into the ground, not negotiated after there's money on the table."
al.fl-p22: By midday he'd reached the covered well at the center of the smaller settlement past the field, the closest thing the area had to a gathering point, a stone trough, a bench worn smooth, and enough shade to draw four or five people at any given hour. By the time he arrived, a small, loose group had already gathered without the formality of the guildhall just a couple days before.
al.fl-p23: An old woman on the bench, resting a basket of greens beside her, looked him over with open curiosity rather than suspicion.
al.fl-p24: "You're the one talking about the research building," she said. "My granddaughter mentioned it. Said something about scientists studying our own soil."
al.fl-p25: "That's the idea," Aldric said, sitting on the low wall across from her. "A regional center, staffed first by people from here, working on what actually matters to Cambria, your soil, your crops, the problems specific to this ground, not whatever other labs find interesting that year."
al.fl-p26: A younger man leaning against the well wall spoke up next.
al.fl-p27: "And if the soil research finds something the big agricultural firms don't like," he asked. "Who does the center actually answer to, when it comes down to it? Because everything eventually answers to somebody."
al.fl-p28: It was a fair question, and a harder one than it sounded.
al.fl-p29: "To a regional board with Cambrian seats holding the majority," he said. "Not to a ministry, and not to whichever firm has the loudest lobbyist that season. If the research says something inconvenient for a large buyer, the center doesn't answer to that buyer. It's the entire point of building it here instead of importing findings from somewhere else."
al.fl-p30: The old woman on the bench nodded slowly and picked her basket back up.
al.fl-p31: "We've had people come through with plans before," she said. "Most of them left with the season. You planning on being one of the ones who comes back?"
al.fl-p32: "I'm planning on it being written down where you can check," Aldric said, "so it doesn't depend on whether I come back or not. Though, I will be here to hear your suggestions and complaints every year."
al.fl-p33: "Fair enough," she said, still thinking about what she was promised.
al.fl-p34: Aldric stayed for a while, long enough that the conversation drifted away from rail and R&D Centers entirely, into the old woman's granddaughter's schooling, the younger man's opinion on a neighboring farm's dispute, small things that had nothing to do with a campaign and everything to do with a small town. Aldric said little during that stretch, mostly listened. He finally rose to walk back toward the road after a while. No one asked him for a slogan on the way out.
al.fl-p35: That, he thought, walking the dirt track back toward the car was probably the more honest measure of the day's work.
al.idx-letter-p1: The tragedy that brought us to this by-election is a shadow over our republic. The loss of MP Richard Balls reminds us of the stakes we face. But while the politicians in Montiablo argue over power, the rural communities of Cambria are left behind.
al.idx-letter-p2: We are watching our local businesses get swallowed by monopolies. We are watching our young people leave because the investment stops at the city limits.
al.idx-letter-p3: I am running because Cambria deserves a voice that cannot be bought. We are going to build a future grounded in our own soil, protected by our own laws, and run by our own people. Join us. Let us rebuild.

ga.idx-hero-title:
Aldric von Reichel
pour Cambria
ga.idx-hero-sub: Une campagne pour les masses laborieuses. Je me bats pour un Cambria où chaque petite entreprise peut prospérer et où chaque ville rurale est connectée - construite sur les fondations indestructibles de notre république.
ga.idx-vision-desc: Notre campagne repose sur des engagements concrets envers la population de Cambria. Qu'il s'agisse de défendre les entreprises locales contre les monopoles et d'obtenir des contrats équitables, ou d'investir dans les infrastructures ferroviaires essentielles et la recherche agricole, nous luttons pour une économie qui donne du pouvoir à chacun.
ga.idx-b1-d: Examens obligatoires des fusions afin de protéger les entreprises locales.
ga.idx-b2-d: Minimums sectoriels pour empêcher les accords prédateurs.
ga.idx-b3-d: Connectivité dédiée construite avec l'autorité de zonage de Cambria.
ga.idx-b4-d: Recherche régionale donnant la priorité aux sols, aux cultures et aux talents locaux.
ga.idx-b5-d: Programmé en fonction des cycles de récolte et de production locaux.
ga.idx-b6-d: Garanties juridiques protégeant le droit d’organisation.
ga.plat-desc: Nous construisons une économie qui récompense ceux qui la construisent, manuellement et mentalement, mettant ainsi fin à l’exploitation par les monopoles.
ga.plat-b1-d: Un examen automatique déclenche les fusions afin de protéger les fournisseurs locaux.
ga.plat-b2-d: Conditions minimales par secteur empêchant les accords prédateurs.
ga.plat-b3-d: Connexion dédiée conservant l’autorité de zonage locale.
ga.plat-b4-d: Centre régional donnant la priorité aux sols, aux cultures et aux talents locaux.
ga.plat-b5-d: Programmé en fonction des cycles de récolte et de production locaux.
ga.plat-b6-d: Une véritable application du droit d’organisation et de grève.
ga.plat-b1-md: "Des gens qui remarqueraient en fait que trois entreprises de livraison n'en font plus qu'une, avant que les formalités administratives ne soient finalisées au lieu d'après, quand il est tard pour les régler... Cette loi ouvrirait plus lentement la porte aux monopoles. Et une porte plus lente, c'est du temps dont vous n'avez pas actuellement."
ga.plat-b2-md: "Conditions contractuelles minimales fixées par secteur, renégociées avec la participation régionale plutôt que dictées par celui qui détient la plus grande part de l'itinéraire en question. Un acheteur peut toujours choisir de ne pas travailler avec vous. Il ne peut pas utiliser l'absence d'alternatives comme levier pour fixer des conditions en dessous de ce plancher."
ga.plat-b3-md: "La connexion va dans les deux sens, sinon elle ne vaut pas la peine d'être construite... Le zonage et le développement autour du nœud restent sous la propre autorité de Cambria, et non confiés à un conseil d'aménagement régional siégeant dans la capitale. Vous obtenez la connexion. Vous ne perdez pas la ville à cause de cela."
ga.plat-b4-md: "Une institution qui répond à cette ville et à ses habitants avant de répondre à qui que ce soit d'autre... Des postes financés ici, réservés en premier aux candidats cambriens, avec des allocations universitaires pour permettre à quelqu'un de cette ville de s'entraîner et de revenir chez lui pour quelque chose qui en vaut la peine."
ga.plat-b5-md: "La capacité de transport de la bourse des aliments pour animaux et de la coopérative textile est calée sur vos cycles de récolte et de production plutôt que sur un horaire fixe à l'échelle de la ville conçu pour la commodité des autres. Un arrêt qui existe parce que Cambria a des marchandises qui valent la peine de s'arrêter, ou du moins je crois que votre cargaison vaut la peine de s'arrêter."
ga.plat-b6-md: "Le monopole ne se soucie pas de savoir si la main qu'il ferme appartient à un propriétaire de magasin ou à un travailleur posté. Il ferme sur les deux."
ga.ev-desc: Suivez Aldric von Reichel sur la campagne électorale alors que nous amenons la plateforme dans tous les coins de Cambria.
ga.ev-card1-d: Aldric von Reichel rencontre la gouvernante Wren et les commerçants locaux au hall de la guilde. Il présente sa plate-forme pour l'application de la loi anti-monopole, la protection des contrats régionaux et l'implantation d'un centre de R&D dédié directement à Cambria.
ga.ev-card2-t: La ligne va dans les deux sens
ga.ev-card2-d: S'adressant aux travailleurs sur le terrain et aux habitants du puits de la ville, Aldric détaille le plan du nœud ferroviaire à grande vitesse. Il s'engage à garantir que le nœud relie Cambria sans céder l'autorité de zonage local aux districts plus grands.

ga.gh-p31: "That's exactly why the order matters," he said. "Funded positions here, reserved for Cambrian applicants first, with the university stipends to make it possible for someone from this town to train and come home to something worth coming home to. Right now the incentive runs one direction, leave, and everything worth having is in Montiablo. This reverses that, even slightly. It gives your children a reason to fill out the application from here instead of in a bigger city."
ga.gh-p32: He continued, before anyone could ask the obvious follow-up.
ga.gh-p33: "And on the rail node, since I promised specifics," he said. "It's not a platform someone builds and forgets. Freight capacity for the feed exchange and the textile cooperative both, timed to your harvest and production cycles rather than a fixed citywide schedule designed around the convenience of others. A stop that exists because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, or so I believe your cargo is worth stopping for."
ga.gh-p34: The feed and grain man's eyebrows rose slightly at that.
ga.gh-p35: "Timed to harvest," he repeated. "Not the others’ schedule stapled onto us."
ga.gh-p36: "Not stapled onto you," Aldric confirmed. "Built around you."
ga.gh-p38: "You understand," she said, "that we have been promised things before, and the people who promised them stopped visiting once the ballots were counted."
ga.gh-p39: "I know," Aldric said. "I'm not asking you to trust the promise. I'm asking you to hold me to it, in this room, next year, and every year after. That's a better offer than trust. It's accountability. And I promise to visit rural town halls every year, so you may hold me accountable and tell your complaints to my face, instead of sending me mails, which I do not really check."
ga.gh-p40: When the meeting broke and the shop owners filed out into the thinning rain, the young man in the apron lingered a moment at the door.
ga.gh-p41: "The research center," he said. "The people running it, actually from here?"

ga.fl-sub: Aldric von Reichel speaks with locals in the fields of Cambria.
ga.fl-p1: The field stretched flat and gold and Aldric von Reichel had left the car at the road rather than have it grind down the access track. He walked the last stretch on foot. By the time he reached the workers stringing wire along a fence line, he had already decided not to open with a speech.
ga.fl-p2: There was no AVIS or any other news agency crew trailing him or any bodyguards. A rural district didn't want to watch itself being performed to, it wanted to see whether a man would still show up when there was no audience to perform for. The road out here had been unpaved for the last two miles, and the dust on his coat was doing more persuasive work than anything he was about to say.
ga.fl-p3: Two men and a woman were working the fence, the kind of task that took a whole morning. The older of the two men, tired, noticed him first and straightened.
ga.fl-p4: "You're the one from the papers," he said. "The SDA man. Didn't expect you'd get your boots dirty for it."
ga.fl-p5: "Seemed dishonest to talk about Cambria from a stage," Aldric said, "when the actual work is out here."
ga.fl-p6: The woman, younger, wiped her hands on her trousers and studied him for a moment.
ga.fl-p7: "We heard about the rail line," she said. "Everyone's heard about the rail line. What we haven't heard is whether it stops here or just goes past us on the way to somewhere that matters more."
ga.fl-p8: "It stops here," Aldric said. "I can walk you to the exact site, if you've got twenty minutes and don't mind the company."
ga.fl-p9: The older man snorted and set down his wire cutters.
ga.fl-p10: "Fence isn't going anywhere," he said. "Go on, then. Show us."
ga.fl-p11: The proposed node sat a fifteen-minute walk further out, a stretch of open ground marked at its corners with survey stakes and faded orange tape. Aldric stopped at the center of it and turned in a slow half circle.
ga.fl-p12: "Here," he said. "Not a platform on the way to one of those big cities. A stop built because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, your grain, your textiles, your goods, and passengers worth carrying both directions instead of just outgoing."
ga.fl-p13: "Both directions," the woman repeated. "Meaning what, exactly? A train that stops so someone from the city can look at us on their way through?"
ga.fl-p14: "Meaning freight capacity timed to your harvest and production cycles," Aldric said. "And meaning it's cheaper and faster for someone in Cambria to reach opportunity in other places without having to become a resident elsewhere to access it. The connection runs both ways, or it isn't worth building."
ga.fl-p15: The older man crouched, picked up a handful of dry soil, and let it run through his fingers, thinking.
ga.fl-p16: "And the town itself," he said. "Because a node like this, I've seen what it does to a place. Suddenly everyone wants to build near it, prices climb, and five years later the town isn't the town anymore. It's a suburb with a station."
ga.fl-p17: Aldric took a moment before answering, giving it weight.
ga.fl-p18: "That's the actual risk," he admitted. "And I won't pretend a rail stop is neutral, it changes a place, always. What I can commit to is that the node connects Cambria to the network without making Cambria's governance answerable to it. No absorption into a larger administrative district built around the station. Zoning and development around the node stay under Cambria's own authority, not handed to a regional planning board seated in the capital. You get the connection. You don't lose the town to it."
ga.fl-p19: The woman folded her arms, weighing it.
ga.fl-p20: "Promises about zoning are easy to make standing in an empty field," she said, not unkindly. "Harder to keep once the surveyors and the money show up."
ga.fl-p21: "Harder," Aldric agreed, "which is exactly why it has to be written into the charter before the first shovel goes into the ground, not negotiated after there's money on the table."
ga.fl-p22: By midday he'd reached the covered well at the center of the smaller settlement past the field, the closest thing the area had to a gathering point, a stone trough, a bench worn smooth, and enough shade to draw four or five people at any given hour. By the time he arrived, a small, loose group had already gathered without the formality of the guildhall just a couple days before.
ga.fl-p23: An old woman on the bench, resting a basket of greens beside her, looked him over with open curiosity rather than suspicion.
ga.fl-p24: "You're the one talking about the research building," she said. "My granddaughter mentioned it. Said something about scientists studying our own soil."
ga.fl-p25: "That's the idea," Aldric said, sitting on the low wall across from her. "A regional center, staffed first by people from here, working on what actually matters to Cambria, your soil, your crops, the problems specific to this ground, not whatever other labs find interesting that year."
ga.fl-p26: A younger man leaning against the well wall spoke up next.
ga.fl-p27: "And if the soil research finds something the big agricultural firms don't like," he asked. "Who does the center actually answer to, when it comes down to it? Because everything eventually answers to somebody."
ga.fl-p28: It was a fair question, and a harder one than it sounded.
ga.fl-p29: "To a regional board with Cambrian seats holding the majority," he said. "Not to a ministry, and not to whichever firm has the loudest lobbyist that season. If the research says something inconvenient for a large buyer, the center doesn't answer to that buyer. It's the entire point of building it here instead of importing findings from somewhere else."
ga.fl-p30: The old woman on the bench nodded slowly and picked her basket back up.
ga.fl-p31: "We've had people come through with plans before," she said. "Most of them left with the season. You planning on being one of the ones who comes back?"
ga.fl-p32: "I'm planning on it being written down where you can check," Aldric said, "so it doesn't depend on whether I come back or not. Though, I will be here to hear your suggestions and complaints every year."
ga.fl-p33: "Fair enough," she said, still thinking about what she was promised.
ga.fl-p34: Aldric stayed for a while, long enough that the conversation drifted away from rail and R&D Centers entirely, into the old woman's granddaughter's schooling, the younger man's opinion on a neighboring farm's dispute, small things that had nothing to do with a campaign and everything to do with a small town. Aldric said little during that stretch, mostly listened. He finally rose to walk back toward the road after a while. No one asked him for a slogan on the way out.
ga.fl-p35: That, he thought, walking the dirt track back toward the car was probably the more honest measure of the day's work.
ga.idx-letter-p1: La tragédie qui nous a amené à cette élection partielle est une ombre sur notre république. La perte du député Richard Balls nous rappelle les enjeux auxquels nous sommes confrontés. Mais tandis que les politiciens de Montiablo se disputent le pouvoir, les communautés rurales de Cambria sont laissées pour compte.
ga.idx-letter-p2: Nous voyons nos entreprises locales se faire engloutir par des monopoles. Nous voyons nos jeunes partir parce que les investissements s'arrêtent aux limites de la ville.
ga.idx-letter-p3: Je me présente parce que Cambria mérite une voix qui ne s'achète pas. Nous allons construire un avenir ancré sur notre propre sol, protégé par nos propres lois et dirigé par notre propre peuple. Rejoignez-nous. Reconstruisons.

ac.idx-hero-title:
Aldric von Reichel
para Cambria
ac.idx-hero-sub: Una campaña para las masas trabajadoras. Estoy luchando por una Cambria donde todas las pequeñas empresas puedan prosperar y todos los pueblos rurales estén conectados, construidos sobre los cimientos inquebrantables de nuestra república.
ac.idx-vision-desc: Nuestra campaña se basa en compromisos tangibles con la gente de Cambria. Desde defender las empresas locales contra los monopolios y garantizar contratos justos hasta invertir en infraestructura ferroviaria crítica e investigación agrícola, luchamos por una economía que empodere a todos.
ac.idx-b1-d: Revisiones obligatorias de fusiones para proteger a las empresas locales.
ac.idx-b2-d: Mínimos sectoriales para evitar acuerdos predatorios.
ac.idx-b3-d: Conectividad dedicada construida con la autoridad de zonificación de Cambria.
ac.idx-b4-d: Investigación regional priorizando suelos, cultivos y talentos locales.
ac.idx-b5-d: Programado según los ciclos locales de cosecha y producción.
ac.idx-b6-d: Salvaguardias legales que protegen el derecho de organización.
ac.plat-desc: Estamos construyendo una economía que recompensa a quienes la construyen, con las manos y con el cerebro, poniendo fin a la explotación de los monopolios.
ac.plat-b1-d: Activadores de revisión automática de fusiones para proteger a los proveedores locales.
ac.plat-b2-d: Plazos mínimos por sector que impidan acuerdos predatorios.
ac.plat-b3-d: Conexión dedicada que conserva la autoridad de zonificación local.
ac.plat-b4-d: Centro regional que prioriza el suelo, los cultivos y el talento locales.
ac.plat-b5-d: Programado según los ciclos locales de cosecha y producción.
ac.plat-b6-d: Aplicación real del derecho de organización y huelga.
ac.plat-b1-md: "Las personas que realmente notarían que tres empresas de reparto se convierten en una, antes de que se finalice el papeleo en lugar de después, cuando ya es tarde para arreglarlo... Esta ley establecería la puerta a los monopolios más lentamente. Y una puerta más lenta es tiempo que no tienen actualmente".
ac.plat-b2-md: "Términos contractuales mínimos establecidos por sector, renegociados con aportaciones regionales en lugar de dictados por quien tenga la mayor parte de la ruta en cuestión. Un comprador aún puede optar por no trabajar con usted. No puede utilizar la ausencia de alternativas como palanca para establecer términos por debajo de ese piso".
ac.plat-b3-md: "La conexión es en ambos sentidos, o no vale la pena construirla... La zonificación y el desarrollo alrededor del nodo quedan bajo la propia autoridad de Cambria, no entregados a una junta de planificación regional con sede en la capital. Se obtiene la conexión. No se pierde la ciudad por ello".
ac.plat-b4-md: "Una institución que responde ante esta ciudad y su gente antes de responder ante cualquier otra persona... Puestos financiados aquí, reservados primero para los solicitantes del Cámbrico, con los estipendios universitarios para hacer posible que alguien de esta ciudad se capacite y regrese a casa y encuentre algo que valga la pena volver a casa".
ac.plat-b5-md: "Capacidad de carga para el intercambio de alimentos y la cooperativa textil, sincronizada con sus ciclos de cosecha y producción en lugar de un cronograma fijo para toda la ciudad diseñado en función de la conveniencia de los demás. Una parada que existe porque Cambria tiene carga por la que vale la pena detenerse, o al menos eso creo que vale la pena detener su carga".
ac.plat-b6-md: "Al monopolio no le importa si la mano que cierra pertenece al dueño de una tienda o a un trabajador por turnos. Cierra a ambos."
ac.ev-desc: Siga a Aldric von Reichel en la campaña electoral mientras llevamos la plataforma a todos los rincones de Cambria.
ac.ev-card1-d: Aldric von Reichel se reúne con la institutriz Wren y los dueños de tiendas locales en el ayuntamiento. Describe su plataforma para la aplicación de las leyes antimonopolio, la protección de contratos regionales y la instalación de un centro de I+D dedicado directamente a Cambria.
ac.ev-card2-t: La línea corre en ambos sentidos
ac.ev-card2-d: Hablando con los trabajadores en el campo y los lugareños en el pozo de la ciudad, Aldric detalla el plan del nodo del Tren de Alta Velocidad. Se compromete a garantizar que el nodo conecte Cambria sin ceder la autoridad de zonificación local a distritos más grandes.
ac.gh-sub: Aldric von Reichel visits the guildhall of Cambria.
ac.gh-p1: The guildhall smelled of old paper, the kind of smell that came from a building that has been actively used by the local community. Aldric von Reichel had walked the market street before coming inside, four shopfronts without any cameras trailing him. A shoemaker had shown him a boot he was proud of. A grocer had complained about delivery contracts that always seemed to favor whoever already had the most trucks. By the time he reached the guildhall, he had heard the shape of the argument he was about to have before anyone in the room had said a word.
ac.gh-p2: The local council of shop owners sat around a long table that had clearly hosted disputes longer and pettier than this one. Twelve chairs, most of them full. At the head sat Governess Wren, a woman who ran the town's only hardware supplier and, by unspoken consensus, ran most other things too.
ac.gh-p3: "Mr. von Reichel," she said, not rising. "You understand we've heard the word 'union' spoken with great affection by your party before. Some of us in this room employ three people. Some of us employ one, ourselves. We are not sure your affection extends to us."
ac.gh-p4: A murmur of agreement went around the table. Aldric remained standing, hands loose at his sides, no notes.
ac.gh-p5: "It doesn't," he said. "Not in the way you mean it. I am not here to organize your one employee against you, Governess. I am here because I think the thing crushing your margins and the thing crushing a factory floor worker's wages in Cambria is, more often than not, the same thing wearing two different coats."
ac.gh-p6: A man near the far end, younger with a hardware apron still on, came forward.
ac.gh-p7: "Go on, then," he said. "Name the reason."
ac.gh-p8: "Consolidation," Aldric said. "Three delivery firms in this region ten years ago. One now. You all know why your contracts stopped being negotiations and turned into their own terms handed down from a corporation building in Montiablo you've never seen. That is monopoly, not to be confused with a fair free market, and monopoly does not care whether the hand it closes belongs to a shop owner or a shift worker. It closes on both."
ac.gh-p9: Governess Wren still not fully satisfied proceeded to ask.
ac.gh-p10: "So what do you propose," she asked. "Beyond a cause we already knew."
ac.gh-p11: "Anti-monopoly enforcement with teeth. Contract protections so a supplier can't be strong-armed by a single buyer with regional leverage. And investment that comes to Cambria instead of past it."
ac.gh-p12: An older woman two seats down from Governess Wren, current owner of the town's textile cooperative, set down her pen.
ac.gh-p13: "Enforcement with teeth," she said. "We've heard that phrase from three different administrations. What does it actually mean? A commission that meets twice a year and writes a report nobody reads? Or something that would have stopped what happened to the delivery firms before it happened?"
ac.gh-p14: Aldric turned to face her directly.
ac.gh-p15: "Before," he said. "a merger or buyout above a set threshold in any sector serving a rural region triggers automatic review, which is different from a simple request. Requests can be quietly avoided by filing under a different registry. Additionally, the review board isn't seated entirely in Montiablo. Regional representatives sit on it. People who'd actually notice three delivery firms becoming one, before the paperwork was finalized instead of after, when it is late to fix it."
ac.gh-p16: The textile cooperative woman considered this, she was no longer dismissive.
ac.gh-p17: "And if the board finds against a merger," she said, "what stops the buyer from simply waiting a season and trying again with better lawyers?"
ac.gh-p18: "Nothing, fully," Aldric admitted. "I won't stand here and tell you a law makes a determined monopolist disappear. But every delay is a season your suppliers keep leverage, you keep alternatives, keep the ability to organize a cooperative buying terms of their own instead of being cornered into one buyer's terms. This law would establish the door into monopolies slower. And a slower door is time you don't currently have."
ac.gh-p19: A heavyset man who had said nothing so far, seated near Wren's right hand finally spoke.
ac.gh-p20: "Contract protections," he said. "Say it plainly. Right now if the one buyer in the region decides my terms next season, I sign or I lose the account entirely, there's no third option. What changes under you?"
ac.gh-p21: "A floor," Aldric said. "Minimum contract terms set by sector, renegotiated with regional input rather than dictated by whoever has the largest share of the route in question. A buyer can still choose not to work with you. They cannot use the absence of alternatives as leverage to set terms below that floor. It removes the worst version of the threat, the negotiation held at your throat."
ac.gh-p22: The man grunted, expressing neither complete agreement nor total dismissal.
ac.gh-p23: "Investment," the man in the apron repeated "Everyone promises investment. Then it's a rail line that runs through us on the way to somewhere else."
ac.gh-p25: "That's fair, and it's the right thing to be suspicious of," he said. "So let me be specific instead of comforting. The high-speed line connects Caprica's regions to each other, however Cambria gets its own node, rather than just a platform on someone else's route. Your town stays as the destination."
ac.gh-p26: He paused, then added the second half, the part he'd been saving.
ac.gh-p27: "And alongside it, a research center. Here. In Cambria, not a simple lecture hall built by Montiablo and staffed by people who know nothing about you and your living. Cambrian scientists, working on what Cambria actually needs, your soil, your supply routes, your workshops, with the freedom to set that agenda themselves. An institution that answers to this town and its people before it answers to anyone else."
ac.gh-p28: The cobbler he'd spoken to earlier that morning, invited to sit in, spoke up from a chair near the back.
ac.gh-p29: "Staffed by Cambrians," he said. "How? You can't conjure scientists out of a market street. Half the young people here leave for Montiablo the day they're old enough to and don't come back."
ac.gh-p30: Aldric thought for a second and responded.
ac.gh-p31: "That's exactly why the order matters," he said. "Funded positions here, reserved for Cambrian applicants first, with the university stipends to make it possible for someone from this town to train and come home to something worth coming home to. Right now the incentive runs one direction, leave, and everything worth having is in Montiablo. This reverses that, even slightly. It gives your children a reason to fill out the application from here instead of in a bigger city."
ac.gh-p32: He continued, before anyone could ask the obvious follow-up.
ac.gh-p33: "And on the rail node, since I promised specifics," he said. "It's not a platform someone builds and forgets. Freight capacity for the feed exchange and the textile cooperative both, timed to your harvest and production cycles rather than a fixed citywide schedule designed around the convenience of others. A stop that exists because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, or so I believe your cargo is worth stopping for."
ac.gh-p34: The feed and grain man's eyebrows rose slightly at that.
ac.gh-p35: "Timed to harvest," he repeated. "Not the others’ schedule stapled onto us."
ac.gh-p36: "Not stapled onto you," Aldric confirmed. "Built around you."
ac.gh-p38: "You understand," she said, "that we have been promised things before, and the people who promised them stopped visiting once the ballots were counted."
ac.gh-p39: "I know," Aldric said. "I'm not asking you to trust the promise. I'm asking you to hold me to it, in this room, next year, and every year after. That's a better offer than trust. It's accountability. And I promise to visit rural town halls every year, so you may hold me accountable and tell your complaints to my face, instead of sending me mails, which I do not really check."
ac.gh-p40: When the meeting broke and the shop owners filed out into the thinning rain, the young man in the apron lingered a moment at the door.
ac.gh-p41: "The research center," he said. "The people running it, actually from here?"
ac.gh-p43: The man considered this, nodded once.

ac.idx-letter-p1: La tragedia que nos trajo a estas elecciones parciales es una sombra sobre nuestra república. La pérdida del diputado Richard Balls nos recuerda lo que está en juego. Pero mientras los políticos de Montiablo discuten por el poder, las comunidades rurales de Cambria quedan atrás.
ac.idx-letter-p2: Estamos viendo cómo los monopolios se tragan a nuestras empresas locales. Estamos viendo a nuestros jóvenes irse porque la inversión se detiene en los límites de la ciudad.
ac.idx-letter-p3: Me postulo porque Cambria merece una voz que no se pueda comprar. Vamos a construir un futuro cimentado en nuestro propio suelo, protegido por nuestras propias leyes y dirigido por nuestra propia gente. Únase a nosotros. Reconstruyamos.

ra.idx-hero-title:
Aldric von Reichel
for Cambria
ra.idx-hero-sub: En kampagne for de arbejdende masser. Jeg kæmper for et Cambria, hvor enhver lille virksomhed kan trives, og hver landby er forbundet - bygget på vores republiks ubrydelige grundlag.
ra.idx-vision-desc: Vores kampagne er bygget på håndgribelige forpligtelser over for befolkningen i Cambria. Fra at forsvare lokale virksomheder mod monopoler og sikre fair kontrakter, til at investere i kritisk jernbaneinfrastruktur og landbrugsforskning, kæmper vi for en økonomi, der styrker alle.
ra.idx-b1-d: Obligatoriske anmeldelser for fusioner for at beskytte lokale virksomheder.
ra.idx-b3-d: Dedikeret forbindelse bygget med Cambrias zonemyndighed.
ra.idx-b4-d: Regional forskning prioriterer lokal jord, afgrøder og talent.
ra.idx-b5-d: Timet til lokale høst- og produktionscyklusser.
ra.idx-b6-d: Juridiske garantier, der beskytter retten til at organisere sig.
ra.plat-desc: Vi opbygger en økonomi, der belønner dem, der bygger den, i hånden og med hjernen, og stopper monopolernes udnyttelse.
ra.plat-b1-d: Automatisk gennemgang udløser fusioner for at beskytte lokale leverandører.
ra.plat-b2-d: Minimumsvilkår fordelt på sektorer, der forhindrer underskudsaftaler.
ra.plat-b3-d: Dedikeret forbindelse, der bevarer lokal zonemyndighed.
ra.plat-b4-d: Regionalt center prioriterer lokal jord, afgrøder og talent.
ra.plat-b5-d: Timet til lokale høst- og produktionscyklusser.
ra.plat-b6-d: Reel håndhævelse bag retten til at organisere sig og strejke.
ra.plat-b1-md: "Folk, der rent faktisk ville bemærke, at tre leveringsfirmaer blev ét, før papirarbejdet blev færdiggjort i stedet for efter, når det er sent at ordne det... Denne lov ville etablere døren til monopoler langsommere. Og en langsommere dør er tid, du ikke har i øjeblikket."
ra.plat-b2-md: "Minimumskontraktvilkår fastsat af sektorer, genforhandlet med regionalt input i stedet for dikteret af den, der har den største andel af den pågældende rute. En køber kan stadig vælge ikke at samarbejde med dig. De kan ikke bruge fraværet af alternativer som løftestang til at sætte vilkår under denne bund."
ra.plat-b3-md: "Forbindelsen forløber begge veje, eller også kan det ikke betale sig at bygge... Zoneinddeling og udvikling omkring knudepunktet bliver under Cambrias egen myndighed, ikke afleveret til en regionsplanbestyrelse, der sidder i hovedstaden. Man får forbindelsen. Man mister ikke byen til det."
ra.plat-b4-md: "En institution, der svarer til denne by og dens folk, før den svarer til nogen anden... Finansierede stillinger her, forbeholdt kambriske ansøgere først, med universitetsstipendier for at gøre det muligt for nogen fra denne by at træne og komme hjem til noget, der er værd at komme hjem til."
ra.plat-b5-md: "Fragtkapacitet for både foderbørsen og tekstilkooperativet, timet til dine høst- og produktionscyklusser i stedet for en fast byplan, der er designet omkring andres bekvemmelighed. Et stop, der eksisterer, fordi Cambria har gods, der er værd at stoppe for, eller så tror jeg, din last er værd at stoppe for."
ra.plat-b6-md: "Monopolet er ligeglad med, om den hånd, det lukker, tilhører en butiksejer eller en skifteholdsarbejder. Den lukker på begge."
ra.ev-desc: Følg Aldric von Reichel på kampagnesporet, mens vi bringer platformen til hvert hjørne af Cambria.
ra.ev-card1-d: Aldric von Reichel mødes med guvernante Wren og lokale butiksejere i guildhallen. Han skitserer sin platform for anti-monopolhåndhævelse, regional kontraktbeskyttelse og at bringe et dedikeret F&U-center direkte til Cambria.
ra.ev-card2-d: Aldric taler med arbejdere i marken og lokalbefolkningen ved bybrønden, og fortæller om nodeplanen for højhastighedstog. Han forpligter sig til at sikre, at knudepunktet forbinder Cambria uden at overgive lokal zonemyndighed til større distrikter.
ra.gh-sub: Aldric von Reichel visits the guildhall of Cambria.
ra.gh-p1: The guildhall smelled of old paper, the kind of smell that came from a building that has been actively used by the local community. Aldric von Reichel had walked the market street before coming inside, four shopfronts without any cameras trailing him. A shoemaker had shown him a boot he was proud of. A grocer had complained about delivery contracts that always seemed to favor whoever already had the most trucks. By the time he reached the guildhall, he had heard the shape of the argument he was about to have before anyone in the room had said a word.
ra.gh-p2: The local council of shop owners sat around a long table that had clearly hosted disputes longer and pettier than this one. Twelve chairs, most of them full. At the head sat Governess Wren, a woman who ran the town's only hardware supplier and, by unspoken consensus, ran most other things too.
ra.gh-p3: "Mr. von Reichel," she said, not rising. "You understand we've heard the word 'union' spoken with great affection by your party before. Some of us in this room employ three people. Some of us employ one, ourselves. We are not sure your affection extends to us."
ra.gh-p4: A murmur of agreement went around the table. Aldric remained standing, hands loose at his sides, no notes.
ra.gh-p5: "It doesn't," he said. "Not in the way you mean it. I am not here to organize your one employee against you, Governess. I am here because I think the thing crushing your margins and the thing crushing a factory floor worker's wages in Cambria is, more often than not, the same thing wearing two different coats."
ra.gh-p6: A man near the far end, younger with a hardware apron still on, came forward.
ra.gh-p7: "Go on, then," he said. "Name the reason."
ra.gh-p8: "Consolidation," Aldric said. "Three delivery firms in this region ten years ago. One now. You all know why your contracts stopped being negotiations and turned into their own terms handed down from a corporation building in Montiablo you've never seen. That is monopoly, not to be confused with a fair free market, and monopoly does not care whether the hand it closes belongs to a shop owner or a shift worker. It closes on both."
ra.gh-p9: Governess Wren still not fully satisfied proceeded to ask.
ra.gh-p10: "So what do you propose," she asked. "Beyond a cause we already knew."
ra.gh-p11: "Anti-monopoly enforcement with teeth. Contract protections so a supplier can't be strong-armed by a single buyer with regional leverage. And investment that comes to Cambria instead of past it."
ra.gh-p12: An older woman two seats down from Governess Wren, current owner of the town's textile cooperative, set down her pen.
ra.gh-p13: "Enforcement with teeth," she said. "We've heard that phrase from three different administrations. What does it actually mean? A commission that meets twice a year and writes a report nobody reads? Or something that would have stopped what happened to the delivery firms before it happened?"
ra.gh-p14: Aldric turned to face her directly.
ra.gh-p15: "Before," he said. "a merger or buyout above a set threshold in any sector serving a rural region triggers automatic review, which is different from a simple request. Requests can be quietly avoided by filing under a different registry. Additionally, the review board isn't seated entirely in Montiablo. Regional representatives sit on it. People who'd actually notice three delivery firms becoming one, before the paperwork was finalized instead of after, when it is late to fix it."
ra.gh-p16: The textile cooperative woman considered this, she was no longer dismissive.
ra.gh-p17: "And if the board finds against a merger," she said, "what stops the buyer from simply waiting a season and trying again with better lawyers?"
ra.gh-p18: "Nothing, fully," Aldric admitted. "I won't stand here and tell you a law makes a determined monopolist disappear. But every delay is a season your suppliers keep leverage, you keep alternatives, keep the ability to organize a cooperative buying terms of their own instead of being cornered into one buyer's terms. This law would establish the door into monopolies slower. And a slower door is time you don't currently have."
ra.gh-p19: A heavyset man who had said nothing so far, seated near Wren's right hand finally spoke.
ra.gh-p20: "Contract protections," he said. "Say it plainly. Right now if the one buyer in the region decides my terms next season, I sign or I lose the account entirely, there's no third option. What changes under you?"
ra.gh-p21: "A floor," Aldric said. "Minimum contract terms set by sector, renegotiated with regional input rather than dictated by whoever has the largest share of the route in question. A buyer can still choose not to work with you. They cannot use the absence of alternatives as leverage to set terms below that floor. It removes the worst version of the threat, the negotiation held at your throat."
ra.gh-p22: The man grunted, expressing neither complete agreement nor total dismissal.
ra.gh-p23: "Investment," the man in the apron repeated "Everyone promises investment. Then it's a rail line that runs through us on the way to somewhere else."
ra.gh-p25: "That's fair, and it's the right thing to be suspicious of," he said. "So let me be specific instead of comforting. The high-speed line connects Caprica's regions to each other, however Cambria gets its own node, rather than just a platform on someone else's route. Your town stays as the destination."
ra.gh-p26: He paused, then added the second half, the part he'd been saving.
ra.gh-p27: "And alongside it, a research center. Here. In Cambria, not a simple lecture hall built by Montiablo and staffed by people who know nothing about you and your living. Cambrian scientists, working on what Cambria actually needs, your soil, your supply routes, your workshops, with the freedom to set that agenda themselves. An institution that answers to this town and its people before it answers to anyone else."
ra.gh-p28: The cobbler he'd spoken to earlier that morning, invited to sit in, spoke up from a chair near the back.
ra.gh-p29: "Staffed by Cambrians," he said. "How? You can't conjure scientists out of a market street. Half the young people here leave for Montiablo the day they're old enough to and don't come back."
ra.gh-p30: Aldric thought for a second and responded.
ra.gh-p31: "That's exactly why the order matters," he said. "Funded positions here, reserved for Cambrian applicants first, with the university stipends to make it possible for someone from this town to train and come home to something worth coming home to. Right now the incentive runs one direction, leave, and everything worth having is in Montiablo. This reverses that, even slightly. It gives your children a reason to fill out the application from here instead of in a bigger city."
ra.gh-p32: He continued, before anyone could ask the obvious follow-up.
ra.gh-p33: "And on the rail node, since I promised specifics," he said. "It's not a platform someone builds and forgets. Freight capacity for the feed exchange and the textile cooperative both, timed to your harvest and production cycles rather than a fixed citywide schedule designed around the convenience of others. A stop that exists because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, or so I believe your cargo is worth stopping for."
ra.gh-p34: The feed and grain man's eyebrows rose slightly at that.
ra.gh-p35: "Timed to harvest," he repeated. "Not the others’ schedule stapled onto us."
ra.gh-p36: "Not stapled onto you," Aldric confirmed. "Built around you."
ra.gh-p38: "You understand," she said, "that we have been promised things before, and the people who promised them stopped visiting once the ballots were counted."
ra.gh-p39: "I know," Aldric said. "I'm not asking you to trust the promise. I'm asking you to hold me to it, in this room, next year, and every year after. That's a better offer than trust. It's accountability. And I promise to visit rural town halls every year, so you may hold me accountable and tell your complaints to my face, instead of sending me mails, which I do not really check."
ra.gh-p40: When the meeting broke and the shop owners filed out into the thinning rain, the young man in the apron lingered a moment at the door.
ra.gh-p41: "The research center," he said. "The people running it, actually from here?"
ra.gh-p43: The man considered this, nodded once.
ra.fl-sub: Aldric von Reichel speaks with locals in the fields of Cambria.
ra.fl-p1: The field stretched flat and gold and Aldric von Reichel had left the car at the road rather than have it grind down the access track. He walked the last stretch on foot. By the time he reached the workers stringing wire along a fence line, he had already decided not to open with a speech.
ra.fl-p2: There was no AVIS or any other news agency crew trailing him or any bodyguards. A rural district didn't want to watch itself being performed to, it wanted to see whether a man would still show up when there was no audience to perform for. The road out here had been unpaved for the last two miles, and the dust on his coat was doing more persuasive work than anything he was about to say.
ra.fl-p3: Two men and a woman were working the fence, the kind of task that took a whole morning. The older of the two men, tired, noticed him first and straightened.
ra.fl-p4: "You're the one from the papers," he said. "The SDA man. Didn't expect you'd get your boots dirty for it."
ra.fl-p5: "Seemed dishonest to talk about Cambria from a stage," Aldric said, "when the actual work is out here."
ra.fl-p6: The woman, younger, wiped her hands on her trousers and studied him for a moment.
ra.fl-p7: "We heard about the rail line," she said. "Everyone's heard about the rail line. What we haven't heard is whether it stops here or just goes past us on the way to somewhere that matters more."
ra.fl-p8: "It stops here," Aldric said. "I can walk you to the exact site, if you've got twenty minutes and don't mind the company."
ra.fl-p9: The older man snorted and set down his wire cutters.
ra.fl-p10: "Fence isn't going anywhere," he said. "Go on, then. Show us."
ra.fl-p11: The proposed node sat a fifteen-minute walk further out, a stretch of open ground marked at its corners with survey stakes and faded orange tape. Aldric stopped at the center of it and turned in a slow half circle.
ra.fl-p12: "Here," he said. "Not a platform on the way to one of those big cities. A stop built because Cambria has cargo worth stopping for, your grain, your textiles, your goods, and passengers worth carrying both directions instead of just outgoing."
ra.fl-p13: "Both directions," the woman repeated. "Meaning what, exactly? A train that stops so someone from the city can look at us on their way through?"
ra.fl-p14: "Meaning freight capacity timed to your harvest and production cycles," Aldric said. "And meaning it's cheaper and faster for someone in Cambria to reach opportunity in other places without having to become a resident elsewhere to access it. The connection runs both ways, or it isn't worth building."
ra.fl-p15: The older man crouched, picked up a handful of dry soil, and let it run through his fingers, thinking.
ra.fl-p16: "And the town itself," he said. "Because a node like this, I've seen what it does to a place. Suddenly everyone wants to build near it, prices climb, and five years later the town isn't the town anymore. It's a suburb with a station."
ra.fl-p17: Aldric took a moment before answering, giving it weight.
ra.fl-p18: "That's the actual risk," he admitted. "And I won't pretend a rail stop is neutral, it changes a place, always. What I can commit to is that the node connects Cambria to the network without making Cambria's governance answerable to it. No absorption into a larger administrative district built around the station. Zoning and development around the node stay under Cambria's own authority, not handed to a regional planning board seated in the capital. You get the connection. You don't lose the town to it."
ra.fl-p19: The woman folded her arms, weighing it.
ra.fl-p20: "Promises about zoning are easy to make standing in an empty field," she said, not unkindly. "Harder to keep once the surveyors and the money show up."
ra.fl-p21: "Harder," Aldric agreed, "which is exactly why it has to be written into the charter before the first shovel goes into the ground, not negotiated after there's money on the table."
ra.fl-p22: By midday he'd reached the covered well at the center of the smaller settlement past the field, the closest thing the area had to a gathering point, a stone trough, a bench worn smooth, and enough shade to draw four or five people at any given hour. By the time he arrived, a small, loose group had already gathered without the formality of the guildhall just a couple days before.
ra.fl-p23: An old woman on the bench, resting a basket of greens beside her, looked him over with open curiosity rather than suspicion.
ra.fl-p24: "You're the one talking about the research building," she said. "My granddaughter mentioned it. Said something about scientists studying our own soil."
ra.fl-p25: "That's the idea," Aldric said, sitting on the low wall across from her. "A regional center, staffed first by people from here, working on what actually matters to Cambria, your soil, your crops, the problems specific to this ground, not whatever other labs find interesting that year."
ra.fl-p26: A younger man leaning against the well wall spoke up next.
ra.fl-p27: "And if the soil research finds something the big agricultural firms don't like," he asked. "Who does the center actually answer to, when it comes down to it? Because everything eventually answers to somebody."
ra.fl-p28: It was a fair question, and a harder one than it sounded.
ra.fl-p29: "To a regional board with Cambrian seats holding the majority," he said. "Not to a ministry, and not to whichever firm has the loudest lobbyist that season. If the research says something inconvenient for a large buyer, the center doesn't answer to that buyer. It's the entire point of building it here instead of importing findings from somewhere else."
ra.fl-p30: The old woman on the bench nodded slowly and picked her basket back up.
ra.fl-p31: "We've had people come through with plans before," she said. "Most of them left with the season. You planning on being one of the ones who comes back?"
ra.fl-p32: "I'm planning on it being written down where you can check," Aldric said, "so it doesn't depend on whether I come back or not. Though, I will be here to hear your suggestions and complaints every year."
ra.fl-p33: "Fair enough," she said, still thinking about what she was promised.
ra.fl-p34: Aldric stayed for a while, long enough that the conversation drifted away from rail and R&D Centers entirely, into the old woman's granddaughter's schooling, the younger man's opinion on a neighboring farm's dispute, small things that had nothing to do with a campaign and everything to do with a small town. Aldric said little during that stretch, mostly listened. He finally rose to walk back toward the road after a while. No one asked him for a slogan on the way out.
ra.fl-p35: That, he thought, walking the dirt track back toward the car was probably the more honest measure of the day's work.
ra.idx-letter-p1: Tragedien, der bragte os til dette mellemvalg, er en skygge over vores republik. Tabet af MP Richard Balls minder os om de indsatser, vi står over for. Men mens politikerne i Montiablo skændes om magten, er landdistrikterne i Cambria efterladt.
ra.idx-letter-p2: Vi ser vores lokale virksomheder blive opslugt af monopoler. Vi ser vores unge gå, fordi investeringen stopper ved bygrænsen.
ra.idx-letter-p3: Jeg stiller op, fordi Cambria fortjener en stemme, der ikke kan købes. Vi skal bygge en fremtid, der er funderet i vores egen jord, beskyttet af vores egne love og styret af vores eget folk. Slut dig til os. Lad os genopbygge.

my.idx-hero-title:
Aldric von Reichel
dros Cambria
my.idx-hero-sub: Ymgyrch ar gyfer y llu sy'n gweithio. Rwy’n ymladd dros Cambria lle gall pob busnes bach ffynnu a phob tref wledig wedi’i chysylltu - wedi’i hadeiladu ar seiliau di-dor ein gweriniaeth.
my.idx-vision-desc: Mae ein hymgyrch yn seiliedig ar ymrwymiadau diriaethol i bobl Cambria. O amddiffyn busnesau lleol yn erbyn monopolïau a sicrhau contractau teg, i fuddsoddi mewn seilwaith rheilffyrdd hanfodol ac ymchwil amaethyddol, rydym yn ymladd dros economi sy’n grymuso pawb.
my.idx-b1-d: Adolygiadau gorfodol ar gyfer uno er mwyn diogelu busnesau lleol.
my.idx-b2-d: Isafswm sector i atal cytundebau rheibus.
my.idx-b3-d: Cysylltedd pwrpasol wedi'i adeiladu gydag awdurdod parthau Cambria.
my.idx-b4-d: Ymchwil rhanbarthol yn blaenoriaethu pridd, cnydau a thalent lleol.
my.idx-b5-d: Wedi'i amseru i gylchoedd cynhaeaf a chynhyrchu lleol.
my.idx-b6-d: Mesurau diogelu cyfreithiol sy'n amddiffyn yr hawl i drefnu.
my.plat-desc: Rydym yn adeiladu economi sy'n gwobrwyo'r rhai sy'n ei adeiladu, â llaw ac ar yr ymennydd, gan roi terfyn ar y camfanteisio gan fonopolïau.
my.plat-b1-d: Sbardunau adolygu awtomatig ar gyfer uno i ddiogelu cyflenwyr lleol.
my.plat-b2-d: Isafswm telerau fesul sector yn atal cytundebau rheibus.
my.plat-b3-d: Cysylltiad pwrpasol sy'n cadw awdurdod parthau lleol.
my.plat-b4-d: Canolfan ranbarthol yn blaenoriaethu pridd, cnydau a thalent lleol.
my.plat-b5-d: Wedi'i amseru i gylchoedd cynhaeaf a chynhyrchu lleol.
my.plat-b6-d: Gorfodaeth go iawn y tu ôl i'r hawl i drefnu a streicio.
my.plat-b1-md: "Pobl a fyddai wedi sylwi ar dri chwmni dosbarthu yn dod yn un, cyn i'r gwaith papur gael ei gwblhau yn lle ar ôl, pan mae'n hwyr i'w drwsio... Byddai'r gyfraith hon yn sefydlu'r drws i fonopolïau'n arafach. Ac mae drws arafach yn amser nad oes gennych chi ar hyn o bryd."
my.plat-b2-md: "Telerau contract gofynnol a bennir fesul sector, wedi'u hailnegodi gyda mewnbwn rhanbarthol yn hytrach na'u pennu gan bwy bynnag sydd â'r gyfran fwyaf o'r llwybr dan sylw. Gall prynwr ddewis peidio â gweithio gyda chi o hyd. Ni allant ddefnyddio absenoldeb dewisiadau eraill fel trosoledd i osod telerau o dan y terfyn isaf hwnnw."
my.plat-b3-md: "Mae'r cysylltiad yn rhedeg y ddwy ffordd, neu nid yw'n werth adeiladu... Mae parthau a datblygu o amgylch y nod yn aros o dan awdurdod Cambria ei hun, heb ei drosglwyddo i fwrdd cynllunio rhanbarthol sy'n eistedd yn y brifddinas. Rydych chi'n cael y cysylltiad. Nid ydych chi'n colli'r dref iddo."
my.plat-b4-md: "Sefydliad sy'n ateb i'r dref hon a'i phobl cyn iddi ateb i unrhyw un arall... Swyddi wedi'u hariannu yma, wedi'u neilltuo ar gyfer ymgeiswyr Cambrian yn gyntaf, gyda chyflogau'r brifysgol i'w gwneud hi'n bosibl i rywun o'r dref hon hyfforddi a dod adref i rywbeth gwerth dod adref."
my.plat-b5-md: "Capasiti cludo nwyddau ar gyfer cyfnewid porthiant a'r cwmni tecstilau cydweithredol ill dau, wedi'i amseru i'ch cylchoedd cynhaeaf a chynhyrchu yn hytrach nag amserlen sefydlog ar gyfer y ddinas gyfan wedi'i chynllunio i fod yn gyfleus i eraill. Stop sy'n bodoli oherwydd bod gan Cambria gargo sy'n werth stopio amdano, neu felly rwy'n credu bod eich cargo yn werth stopio amdano."
my.plat-b6-md: "Nid yw monopoli yn poeni a yw'r llaw y mae'n ei chau yn perthyn i berchennog siop neu weithiwr shifft. Mae'n cau ar y ddau."
my.ev-desc: Dilynwch Aldric von Reichel ar lwybr yr ymgyrch wrth i ni ddod â’r platfform i bob cornel o Cambria.
my.ev-card1-d: Aldric von Reichel yn cyfarfod â Governess Wren a pherchnogion siopau lleol yn y guildhall. Mae'n amlinellu ei lwyfan ar gyfer gorfodi gwrth-fonopoli, amddiffyniadau contract rhanbarthol, a dod â chanolfan ymchwil a datblygu bwrpasol yn uniongyrchol i Cambria.
my.ev-card2-t: Mae'r Lein yn Rhedeg y Ddwy Ffordd
my.ev-card2-d: Wrth siarad â gweithwyr yn y maes a phobl leol yn ffynnon y dref, mae Aldric yn manylu ar gynllun nodau High-Speed ​​Rail. Mae'n ymrwymo i sicrhau bod y nod yn cysylltu Cambria heb ildio awdurdod parthau lleol i ardaloedd mwy.

my.gh-p9: Governess Wren still not fully satisfied proceeded to ask.
my.gh-p10: "So what do you propose," she asked. "Beyond a cause we already knew."
my.gh-p11: "Anti-monopoly enforcement with teeth. Contract protections so a supplier can't be strong-armed by a single buyer with regional leverage. And investment that comes to Cambria instead of past it."
my.gh-p12: An older woman two seats down from Governess Wren, current owner of the town's textile cooperative, set down her pen.
my.gh-p13: "Enforcement with teeth," she said. "We've heard that phrase from three different administrations. What does it actually mean? my.gh-p14: Aldric turned to face her directly.
my.gh-p15: "Before," he said. "a merger or buyout above a set threshold in any sector serving a rural region triggers automatic review, which is different from a simple request. my.gh-p16: The textile cooperative woman considered this, she was no longer dismissive.
my.gh-p17: "And if the board finds against a merger," she said, "what stops the buyer from simply waiting a season and trying again with better lawyers?"
my.gh-p18: "Nothing, fully," Aldric admitted. "I won't stand here and tell you a law makes a determined monopolist disappear. my.gh-p20: "Contract protections," he said. "Say it plainly. Right now if the one buyer in the region decides my terms next season, I sign or I lose the account entirely, there's no third option. What changes under you?"
